questions.prompt: A student has two hours to study and can either review for an economics exam (gaining 30 expected points) or a history exam (gaining 24 expected points). What is the opportunity cost of studying economics for the full two hours?
choices: 30 economics exam points
24 history exam points
6 history exam points
Two hours of leisure time
questions.correctText: 24 history exam points
questions.explanation: Opportunity cost is the value of the next-best alternative forgone. By spending all two hours on economics, the student gives up the 24 history points they could have earned instead.

questions.prompt: An economy is currently operating at a point inside its production possibilities curve (PPC). Which conclusion follows most directly?
choices: Resources are being used efficiently at full employment.
Some resources are unemployed or underutilized.
The economy has experienced long-run economic growth.
The PPC has shifted inward due to a decline in technology.
questions.correctText: Some resources are unemployed or underutilized.
questions.explanation: Points inside the PPC represent inefficient or underemployed production. Points on the curve are efficient; points beyond it are currently unattainable without growth.

questions.prompt: Country A can produce either 40 bushels of wheat or 20 barrels of oil with its resources. Country B can produce either 30 bushels of wheat or 30 barrels of oil. Which country has a comparative advantage in oil?
choices: Country A, because its opportunity cost of oil is 2 wheat per barrel.
Country B, because its opportunity cost of oil is 1 wheat per barrel.
Country A, because it can produce more wheat overall.
Neither country, because their PPCs are linear.
questions.correctText: Country B, because its opportunity cost of oil is 1 wheat per barrel.
questions.explanation: Country A gives up 2 wheat for each barrel of oil (40/20); Country B gives up only 1 wheat per barrel (30/30). The lower opportunity cost producer has the comparative advantage.

questions.prompt: In the circular flow model of a closed economy with no government, which of the following best describes the flow from households to firms?
choices: Goods and services in the product market and tax payments in the input market.
Factors of production in the resource market and spending in the product market.
Wages, rent, and interest in the resource market.
Government transfer payments and subsidies.
questions.correctText: Factors of production in the resource market and spending in the product market.
questions.explanation: Households supply land, labor, and capital to firms through the resource market and exchange income for goods and services through the product market. Firms send goods one way and factor payments the other.

questions.prompt: Which of the following would cause a country's production possibilities curve to shift outward?
choices: A decrease in the unemployment rate from 7% to 4%.
An increase in the rate at which consumers spend on imported goods.
A technological breakthrough that doubles labor productivity.
A reallocation of workers from one industry to another.
questions.correctText: A technological breakthrough that doubles labor productivity.
questions.explanation: Outward PPC shifts come from genuine economic growth: more resources, better technology, or higher productivity. Lower unemployment moves production from inside the PPC toward the curve but does not shift the curve itself.

questions.prompt: Which of the following transactions would be counted in this year's U.S. GDP?
choices: A used car sold by one neighbor to another for $7,000.
A stockbroker's $400 commission on a stock trade executed this year.
The resale of a 1990s vintage guitar at an antique shop.
Cash income that a babysitter received and did not report.
questions.correctText: A stockbroker's $400 commission on a stock trade executed this year.
questions.explanation: GDP counts final goods and services newly produced this year. Used goods and unreported income are excluded. The stockbroker's commission is a newly produced financial service and is included.

questions.prompt: Nominal GDP in Year 2 is $22 trillion and the GDP deflator is 110 (Year 1 = 100). What is real GDP in Year 2, measured in Year 1 prices?
choices: $20 trillion
$22 trillion
$24.2 trillion
$11 trillion
questions.correctText: $20 trillion
questions.explanation: Real GDP = Nominal GDP / (Deflator/100) = 22 / 1.10 = $20 trillion. Dividing by the deflator strips out price changes and isolates real output growth.

questions.prompt: A laid-off auto worker who is actively interviewing for new jobs is best classified as which type of unemployment?
choices: Frictional, because she is between jobs.
Structural, because her skills are obsolete.
Cyclical, because the layoff results from a downturn in the business cycle.
Seasonal, because auto sales vary throughout the year.
questions.correctText: Cyclical, because the layoff results from a downturn in the business cycle.
questions.explanation: Cyclical unemployment is caused by recessions and falling aggregate demand. Frictional involves voluntary searching; structural involves a permanent mismatch of skills.

questions.prompt: If the Consumer Price Index (CPI) rises from 240 to 252 over one year, what is the annual inflation rate?
questions.explanation: Inflation rate = (252 - 240) / 240 x 100 = 12/240 = 5.0%. The CPI measures the price level of a fixed basket of consumer goods relative to a base year.

questions.prompt: Which group is most clearly harmed by unanticipated inflation?
choices: Borrowers who locked in a fixed nominal interest rate before the inflation.
Lenders who locked in a fixed nominal interest rate before the inflation.
Homeowners with adjustable-rate mortgages.
Workers with strong cost-of-living adjustment clauses in their contracts.
questions.correctText: Lenders who locked in a fixed nominal interest rate before the inflation.
questions.explanation: Unanticipated inflation transfers wealth from lenders to borrowers because debts are repaid in dollars worth less than expected. The real interest rate the lender receives is lower than they planned for.

questions.prompt: An economy's labor force is 160 million and 12 million people are unemployed. What is the unemployment rate?
questions.explanation: Unemployment rate = unemployed / labor force x 100 = 12 / 160 x 100 = 7.5%. The labor force includes both the employed and those actively seeking work.

questions.prompt: A worker who lost manufacturing jobs to automation and now lacks the digital skills demanded by employers is best described as which type of unemployment?
choices: Frictional
Cyclical
Structural
Seasonal
questions.correctText: Structural
questions.explanation: Structural unemployment occurs when workers' skills no longer match the available jobs because of changes in technology or industry composition. Retraining is typically required.

questions.prompt: During the expansion phase of a typical business cycle, which set of changes is most likely to occur?
choices: Real GDP rising, unemployment falling, and inflation tending to rise.
Real GDP falling, unemployment rising, and inflation rising.
Real GDP rising, unemployment rising, and inflation falling.
Real GDP unchanged, unemployment falling, and prices unchanged.
questions.correctText: Real GDP rising, unemployment falling, and inflation tending to rise.
questions.explanation: Expansions feature rising output, falling joblessness, and upward pressure on prices as the economy approaches its potential. Contractions reverse these patterns.

questions.prompt: A sudden and large increase in consumer optimism is most likely to shift which curve in which direction in the short run?
choices: Aggregate demand to the left.
Aggregate demand to the right.
Short-run aggregate supply to the left.
Short-run aggregate supply to the right.
questions.correctText: Aggregate demand to the right.
questions.explanation: Optimistic consumers raise consumption spending at every price level, shifting AD rightward. In the short run, this increases both real output and the price level.

questions.prompt: An economy in short-run equilibrium has actual real GDP of $19 trillion while full-employment real GDP is $20 trillion. This economy is experiencing:
choices: An inflationary gap of $1 trillion.
A recessionary gap of $1 trillion.
Long-run equilibrium with no output gap.
Stagflation with rising prices and falling output.
questions.correctText: A recessionary gap of $1 trillion.
questions.explanation: When actual output is below potential, the economy has a recessionary gap. Unemployment exceeds the natural rate, and policy responses such as expansionary fiscal or monetary policy may be used.

questions.prompt: If the marginal propensity to consume (MPC) is 0.75, what is the value of the simple spending multiplier?
questions.explanation: The spending multiplier equals 1 / (1 - MPC) = 1 / (1 - 0.75) = 1 / 0.25 = 4. A $1 increase in autonomous spending raises equilibrium real GDP by $4.

questions.prompt: An economy has an MPC of 0.8 and a recessionary gap of $500 billion. Using the simple spending multiplier, how much new government spending is needed to close the gap?
choices: $100 billion
$400 billion
$500 billion
$2,500 billion
questions.correctText: $100 billion
questions.explanation: Multiplier = 1 / (1 - 0.8) = 5. Required spending = gap / multiplier = $500B / 5 = $100B. Each dollar of government purchases ultimately generates $5 of new real GDP.

questions.prompt: A severe drought sharply reduces agricultural output and raises food prices. In the AD/AS model, this is best represented as:
choices: A rightward shift of aggregate demand.
A leftward shift of short-run aggregate supply.
A movement along a stationary short-run aggregate supply curve.
A rightward shift of long-run aggregate supply.
questions.correctText: A leftward shift of short-run aggregate supply.
questions.explanation: A negative supply shock raises production costs and shifts SRAS leftward, producing stagflation: higher prices and lower real GDP simultaneously.

questions.prompt: Which statement best describes the short-run Phillips curve?
choices: There is a positive relationship between inflation and unemployment.
There is an inverse relationship between inflation and unemployment.
Unemployment always returns to its natural rate, regardless of inflation.
Inflation is unrelated to changes in aggregate demand.
questions.correctText: There is an inverse relationship between inflation and unemployment.
questions.explanation: Along a short-run Phillips curve, higher inflation is associated with lower unemployment. The relationship reflects movements along the SRAS curve driven by AD shifts.

questions.prompt: An economy is in long-run equilibrium when:
choices: AD intersects SRAS to the right of full-employment output.
AD intersects SRAS to the left of full-employment output.
AD, SRAS, and LRAS all intersect at the same point.
The price level equals zero and unemployment equals zero.
questions.correctText: AD, SRAS, and LRAS all intersect at the same point.
questions.explanation: In long-run equilibrium, output equals potential GDP (LRAS), there is no output gap, and the actual price level equals the expected price level. Unemployment equals its natural rate.

questions.prompt: An economy currently has an inflationary gap. In the absence of any policy response, how does it self-correct in the long run?
choices: AD shifts left as consumers spend less.
SRAS shifts left as nominal wages and input prices rise.
LRAS shifts right as technology improves.
AD shifts right as inflation expectations rise.
questions.correctText: SRAS shifts left as nominal wages and input prices rise.
questions.explanation: With output above potential, labor markets are tight; workers eventually negotiate higher nominal wages. Rising costs push SRAS left until output returns to LRAS at a higher price level.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is included in M1 but not in the narrower category of currency in circulation?
choices: Savings deposits at commercial banks.
Funds in checkable demand-deposit accounts.
Small-denomination time deposits.
Money market mutual fund shares held by individuals.
questions.correctText: Funds in checkable demand-deposit accounts.
questions.explanation: M1 includes currency in circulation plus checkable deposits and traveler's checks. Savings deposits, small time deposits, and retail money market funds are part of the broader M2 aggregate.

questions.prompt: If the required reserve ratio is 10% and the banking system receives a new $1,000 cash deposit, what is the maximum total expansion of the money supply (including the original deposit), assuming no leakages?
questions.explanation: Money multiplier = 1 / reserve ratio = 1 / 0.10 = 10. Maximum expansion = $1,000 x 10 = $10,000 in total deposits, including the original deposit.

questions.prompt: Which Federal Reserve action would most directly increase the money supply?
choices: Selling Treasury bonds on the open market.
Raising the discount rate at which it lends to banks.
Buying Treasury bonds from commercial banks.
Raising the reserve requirement.
questions.correctText: Buying Treasury bonds from commercial banks.
questions.explanation: When the Fed buys bonds, it credits bank reserves, increasing the monetary base. Banks can then create new loans, expanding the money supply through the multiplier process.

questions.prompt: In the money market, an increase in real GDP would most directly cause:
choices: A leftward shift of money demand and a lower nominal interest rate.
A rightward shift of money demand and a higher nominal interest rate.
A rightward shift of money supply and a lower nominal interest rate.
A movement along money demand with no change in the interest rate.
questions.correctText: A rightward shift of money demand and a higher nominal interest rate.
questions.explanation: Higher real GDP raises the volume of transactions, increasing the demand for money at every interest rate. With a vertical money supply, the equilibrium nominal interest rate rises.

questions.prompt: In the loanable funds market, an increase in private saving by households will most directly cause:
choices: A rightward shift of the supply of loanable funds and a lower real interest rate.
A leftward shift of the supply of loanable funds and a higher real interest rate.
A rightward shift of the demand for loanable funds and a higher real interest rate.
No change in either curve.
questions.correctText: A rightward shift of the supply of loanable funds and a lower real interest rate.
questions.explanation: Greater saving expands the supply of funds available to be lent. With unchanged demand, the equilibrium real interest rate falls and the quantity of investment rises.

questions.prompt: What is the most likely effect of a Federal Reserve open-market purchase of bonds on bond prices and short-term nominal interest rates?
choices: Bond prices fall, and short-term interest rates rise.
Bond prices rise, and short-term interest rates fall.
Bond prices rise, and short-term interest rates rise.
Both bond prices and short-term interest rates are unaffected.
questions.correctText: Bond prices rise, and short-term interest rates fall.
questions.explanation: Fed bond buying raises the price of bonds (demand up). Bond prices and yields move inversely, so yields and short-term interest rates fall. This is the standard transmission of expansionary monetary policy.

questions.prompt: Suppose banks decide to hold more excess reserves rather than lend them out. What happens to the actual money multiplier?
choices: It rises, because reserves count fully in the money supply.
It falls, because a smaller share of reserves is loaned out.
It is unaffected, since the multiplier depends only on the reserve requirement.
It becomes negative.
questions.correctText: It falls, because a smaller share of reserves is loaned out.
questions.explanation: Excess reserves and cash leakages dampen money creation. The actual multiplier is always less than 1 / reserve ratio when banks hold excess reserves or the public holds cash.

questions.prompt: Which combination of policies is most expansionary?
choices: Tax increases and open-market sales of bonds.
Tax cuts and open-market purchases of bonds.
Tax cuts and open-market sales of bonds.
Tax increases and open-market purchases of bonds.
questions.correctText: Tax cuts and open-market purchases of bonds.
questions.explanation: Tax cuts increase disposable income and consumption (expansionary fiscal); open-market purchases lower interest rates and raise investment (expansionary monetary). Both shift AD right.

questions.prompt: A large deficit-financed increase in government spending is most likely to cause crowding out by:
choices: Raising the real interest rate and reducing private investment.
Lowering the real interest rate and increasing private investment.
Raising private saving and decreasing the demand for loanable funds.
Increasing net exports through a weaker domestic currency.
questions.correctText: Raising the real interest rate and reducing private investment.
questions.explanation: When the government borrows more, demand for loanable funds rises, pushing real interest rates up. Higher rates reduce interest-sensitive private investment, offsetting some of the fiscal stimulus.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is most likely to promote long-run economic growth by shifting the long-run aggregate supply curve to the right?
choices: A temporary tax rebate that boosts consumption for one quarter.
A sustained increase in spending on education and worker training.
An increase in unemployment insurance benefits.
An expansionary monetary policy that lowers nominal interest rates.
questions.correctText: A sustained increase in spending on education and worker training.
questions.explanation: Investment in human capital raises labor productivity and expands an economy's productive capacity, shifting LRAS right. Demand-side policies and transfers do not affect potential output.

questions.prompt: According to the classical/long-run view of money, a permanent doubling of the money supply (holding real factors constant) will primarily:
choices: Double real GDP without changing the price level.
Double the price level without changing real GDP.
Cut real GDP in half while leaving the price level unchanged.
Permanently lower the unemployment rate.
questions.correctText: Double the price level without changing real GDP.
questions.explanation: This is the long-run neutrality of money. Output is determined by real factors (technology, labor, capital). Changes in the money supply ultimately change only nominal variables such as the price level.

questions.prompt: Which is the best example of an automatic fiscal stabilizer in action during a recession?
choices: Congress passing a new infrastructure law six months into the downturn.
Unemployment insurance payments rising as layoffs increase, with no new legislation.
The Federal Reserve lowering the federal funds rate target.
The Treasury issuing more long-term bonds.
questions.correctText: Unemployment insurance payments rising as layoffs increase, with no new legislation.
questions.explanation: Automatic stabilizers, like UI and progressive income taxes, expand and contract with the cycle without requiring new legislation. They dampen swings in disposable income and aggregate demand.

questions.prompt: If an economy is in a long-run equilibrium and the central bank permanently increases the money supply, what happens in the long run?
choices: Real GDP rises permanently above potential output.
The price level rises and real GDP returns to potential output.
The unemployment rate falls permanently below the natural rate.
The real interest rate falls permanently.
questions.correctText: The price level rises and real GDP returns to potential output.
questions.explanation: In the short run, expansionary monetary policy can raise real GDP. In the long run, nominal wages and prices adjust; output returns to potential at a higher price level. Money is neutral in the long run.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is recorded as a debit (an outflow) in the current account of the U.S. balance of payments?
choices: A U.S. household purchases a Japanese-made car from a dealer in the U.S.
A German firm purchases U.S.-made aircraft and ships them to Frankfurt.
A foreign tourist spends money at a hotel in New York City.
A U.S. firm receives interest income on bonds it owns in the U.K.
questions.correctText: A U.S. household purchases a Japanese-made car from a dealer in the U.S.
questions.explanation: Imports of goods are debits in the current account. Exports, foreign tourist spending in the U.S., and income earned by U.S. residents abroad are credits.

questions.prompt: If U.S. real interest rates rise relative to those in the eurozone, holding other factors constant, which is most likely to occur?
choices: The demand for U.S. dollars in the foreign exchange market falls, and the dollar depreciates.
The demand for U.S. dollars in the foreign exchange market rises, and the dollar appreciates.
The supply of U.S. dollars rises, and the dollar appreciates.
There is no effect on the exchange rate, only on U.S. domestic interest rates.
questions.correctText: The demand for U.S. dollars in the foreign exchange market rises, and the dollar appreciates.
questions.explanation: Higher U.S. real rates attract foreign financial capital. Foreigners demand more dollars to buy U.S. assets, shifting dollar demand right and causing the dollar to appreciate against the euro.

questions.prompt: If the U.S. dollar appreciates significantly against the Mexican peso, the most likely short-run effects on U.S. trade with Mexico are:
choices: U.S. exports to Mexico rise and U.S. imports from Mexico fall.
U.S. exports to Mexico fall and U.S. imports from Mexico rise.
Both U.S. exports and imports fall.
Both U.S. exports and imports rise.
questions.correctText: U.S. exports to Mexico fall and U.S. imports from Mexico rise.
questions.explanation: A stronger dollar makes U.S. goods more expensive for Mexican buyers (exports fall) and makes Mexican goods cheaper for U.S. buyers (imports rise). Net exports decrease.

questions.prompt: Under a fixed exchange rate regime, suppose the domestic currency is pegged above the free-market equilibrium rate (overvalued). To maintain the peg, the central bank must:
choices: Buy its own currency in the foreign exchange market using foreign reserves.
Sell its own currency in exchange for foreign reserves.
Lower the discount rate to encourage capital outflows.
Do nothing; market forces will keep the rate at the peg.
questions.correctText: Buy its own currency in the foreign exchange market using foreign reserves.
questions.explanation: An overvalued peg implies surplus supply of the domestic currency at the official rate. The central bank must buy up that surplus with foreign reserves, propping up the price.

questions.prompt: An expansionary U.S. monetary policy that lowers the U.S. real interest rate is most likely to affect the U.S. net export schedule by:
choices: Causing the dollar to appreciate and net exports to fall.
Causing the dollar to depreciate and net exports to rise.
Causing the dollar to appreciate and net exports to rise.
Leaving the dollar and net exports unchanged.
questions.correctText: Causing the dollar to depreciate and net exports to rise.
questions.explanation: Lower U.S. real rates make U.S. assets less attractive, reducing demand for dollars and depreciating the currency. A weaker dollar makes U.S. exports cheaper abroad and imports pricier at home, raising net exports.
